sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 78, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'Moves character right by 3 cols', 'DOWN': 'Moves character down by 3 rows', 'LEFT': 'Moves character left by 3 cols', 'UP': 'Moves character up by 3 rows', 'SPACE': 'Rotates/transforms character'}
- Object taxonomy: {'white_shape': 'Player character (P-shape)', 'red_squares': '3x3 red blocks', 'green_frame': 'Green frame template (P-shape)', 'gray_blocks': 'Gray blocks from interactions'}
- HUD map: {'top_bar': 'charcoal bar at row 0'}
- Win pattern: Unknown - game over reached without completing level
- Level log: {'level_1': 'Game over at step 76, score 0. Did not complete level.'}
Current level:
- World model: Game over state. Character is white P-shape. Green frame is also P-shape. Red blocks are 3x3 squares. Gray blocks appear from interactions.
- Goal model: Fit character into green frame by rotating and moving
- Recent findings: ['Character shape changes with rotation (SPACE)', 'Character moves in 3-cell increments (UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT)']
- Open questions: ['What exact configuration completes the level?']
- Plan: Game over - no more actions possible
- Strategy log: ['Cycling through orientations and moving character did not complete level - game over reached']
- Failed probes: ['Multiple SPACE rotations without completing level', 'Multiple UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT moves without completing level']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 77 actions; a strong score needs about 29 or fewer. You are 2.7x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 76 actions returned the board to a state already seen 76 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 76 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game is still active (step 78, not game over anymore). The RIGHT action worked and moved the character. Now I need to figure out the correct solution.
Looking at the current image:
- White P-shape character at top left (around rows 11-25, cols 11-25)
- Green P-frame at bottom right (around rows 29-49, cols 41-49)
- Red blocks below the white character
The goal seems to be to move the white P-shape to fit into the green P-frame. The green frame is a P-shape outline that the character needs to fill.
From the previous output:
- White character at: [[11, 11], [11, 25]]
- Green frame at: [[29, 41], [29, 49]]
So the white character is at rows 11-25, cols 11-25 (approximately).
The green frame is at rows 29-49, cols 41-49 (approximately).
To move from the white character's position to the green frame's position:
- Need to move RIGHT: (41-11)/3 ≈ 10 moves
- Need to move DOWN: (29-11)/3 ≈ 6 moves
But wait, I need to be more precise. Let me check the current positions after the RIGHT action.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully:
- The white P-shape is at the top left
- The green P-frame is at the bottom right
- The red blocks are below the white character
The green frame appears to be a P-shape that's oriented differently from the white character. Looking at the green frame, it seems like it's a P-shape that's rotated 180 degrees (upside down P) or maybe it's a mirrored P.
Let me think about what the correct solution would be:
1. Move the white character to the green frame's position
2. Rotate the character to match the green frame's orientation
But I need to be more precise about the positions and orientations. Let me check the current state after the RIGHT action.
Actually, looking at the current image, the white character has moved to the right (from the RIGHT action). Now I need to continue moving it to the green frame's position.
Let me calculate the exact number of moves needed:
- White character: around (11-25, 11-25)
- Green frame: around (29-49, 41-49)
To move from col 11 to col 41: (41-11)/3 = 10 RIGHT moves
To move from row 11 to row 29: (29-11)/3 = 6 DOWN moves
But I also need to consider the orientation. The white character is an upright P, and the green frame appears to be a P-shape that's oriented differently.
Let me try to move the character to the green frame's position and see what happens. I'll batch the moves to be efficient.
Actually, wait. Looking at the green frame more carefully, it seems like it's a P-shape that's oriented with the opening facing left (like a backwards P). The white character is an upright P with the opening facing right.
So I might need to rotate the character to match the green frame's orientation. Let me try:
1. Move RIGHT several times to align horizontally
2. Move DOWN several times to align vertically
3. Rotate with SPACE to match the orientation

Looking at the green frame:

- The white character is an upright P with the opening facing right
To match the green frame's orientation, I need to rotate the white character 180 degrees (or flip it).

2. Move DOWN several times to align vertically
3. Rotate with SPACE to match the orientation

2. Rotate to match the orientation
3. Check if the level is completed

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current positions after RIGHT action
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

# Find white character and green frame
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] > 50:
        print(f"White: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary corners: {n['boundary'][:4]}")
    if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] > 50:
        print(f"Green: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary corners: {n['boundary'][:4]}")

# Calculate moves needed
# White at ~row 11-25, col 11-25 (after RIGHT, col shifted by 3)
# Green at ~row 29-49, col 41-49
# Need RIGHT: (41-14)/3 ≈ 9 more (already did 1)
# Need DOWN: (29-11)/3 ≈ 6

# Let's batch moves to get close to green frame
# First, move RIGHT several times, then DOWN
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "Moves character right by 3 cols", "DOWN": "Moves character down by 3 rows", "LEFT": "Moves character left by 3 cols", "UP": "Moves character up by 3 rows", "SPACE": "Rotates/transforms character"}, "object_taxonomy": {"white_shape": "Player character (P-shape)", "red_squares": "3x3 red blocks", "green_frame": "Green frame template (P-shape)", "gray_blocks": "Gray blocks from interactions"}, "hud_map": {"top_bar": "charcoal bar at row 0"}, "win_pattern": "Fit white P-shape into green P-frame by moving and rotating", "level_log": {"level_1": "Game over at step 76, now recovered. Need to move character to green frame."}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "White P-shape at top-left, green P-frame at bottom-right. Character moves 3 cells per action.", "goal_model": "Move character to green frame position and rotate to match orientation", "recent_findings": ["RIGHT action moved character, game is active again"], "open_questions": ["What rotation matches green frame?"], "current_plan": "Move RIGHT 9 times, then DOWN 6 times to reach green frame", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
